title: Talking to Strangers
author: Malcolm Gladwell
review: Malcolm Gladwell is one of the best nonfiction writers out there. His perspective of the societal failures that explain the Sandra Bland case are unique and complex, yet he perfectly articulates his argument. I enjoyed learning about "defaulting to the truth" and transparency to explain human behaviors, and I'm intrigued by the theory of coupling to explain gun violence and other forms of death. Overall extremely digestible and Gladwell's writing is one of my favorite ways to consume knowledge!!!

title: Cyberspies: The Secret History of Surveillance, Hacking, and Digital Espionage
author: Gordon Corera
review: Omg I nerded out so much!!! I was making so many connections to so many cs and polysci classes which made me so happy I can't wait to take my classes this semester and learn even more!!! This book discusses so many cool topics from the Cold War to how the internet started to corporate and state scandals. Corera did an excellent job outlining the history of computer infiltration and security, which can be quite difficult when combining historical and technical contexts. I was enlightened by his explanation of the corporate incentive to not invest in cyber security practices- something I've been confused about for a while. I was particularly intrigued by his British perspective of the US government and its operations. Overall great book but def has me concerned about digital privacy.

title: Love and Other Words
author: Christina Lauren
review: One of the best romance books I've read in a while!! I'm a sucker for friends to lovers trope :) Isa rec and main characters best friend is named Sabrina epic book!

title: Troubled: A Memoir of Foster Care, Family, and Social Class
author: Rob Henderson
review: Verity mom rec!! Super interesting book- I was particularly intrigued about how his life experiences shaped his criticisms of the higher education system. I thought some of his conclusions were a bit far fetched based on my personal experiences, but the political climate is different at Yale and Vandy. Overall enjoyed his reading his argument and learning about his life story!

title: The Sun Is Also a Star
author: Nicola Yoon

title: Happy Place
author: Emily Henry
review: Isa rec!! Such a cute wholesome book- very relatable (main character's best friend is named Sabrina)! I love a happy ending :)

title: I Am Malala
author: Malala Yousafzai
review: One of my favorite books so I had to read it again. Malala's story is incredibly inspiring (I cried)

title: All About Love: New Visions
author: bell hooks
review: Every sentence bell hooks wrote in this book was profound and life changing; I simply cannot choose a favorite quote to leave in this review. This book made me think about love (and adjacent topics) from an entirely different perspective, changing quite a few of my opinions. Because of hooks' unique feminist perspective on love, ability to share vulnerable life experiences, and extensive research, I believe all who read this book will walk away with incredible insight into life, happiness, and relationships.

title: Tuesdays with Morrie
author: Mitch Albom
review: My new favorite book. Sobbed the entire time.

title: Cuentos para pensar
author: Jorge Bucay
review: My host mom gave me this book to practice my Spanish and it was a great learning tool. I enjoyed the short stories because they made me reflect, but I thought they were quite basic in terms of the life lessons that each highlighted and didn't offer any particularly unique insights or angles.

title: Turtles All the Way Down

review: Idk why I waited this long to read this book, john green will definitely be in my future

title: When God Winks at You

review: One of my favorite books from the 5th grade and one of the reasons I believe in God!!!

review: Isabella rec! I know so many things about octopuses now! Author is an incredible writer

title: The Subtle Art of Not Giving a F*ck

title: You Look Like a Thing and I Love You
author: Janelle Shane
review: Overall good book, author explained a complex topic through digestible examples. However she focused mainly on introductory level concepts that I was already familiar with so I didn't learn a lot :( Saw a few examples that I used in my college essays!!

review: Mason rec!

title: The Giver of Stars
author: Jojo Moyes
review: This book is about a packhorse library in the mid 1900s in Eastern Ky. It's a super sweet novel, very wholesome but nothing profound. I love Margary's character development throughout.

title: The Paradise Problem
author: Christina Lauren
review: Very cute book! One of my fav tropes, I'm back in my romance book era

title: Martyr!
author: Kaveh Akbar
review: Martyr! might be one of the strangest, most intellectual books I've ever read. The protagonist is this witty Iranian guy that's fixated on the idea of martyrdom and death in general, and obsessively pursues this because he believes it's the only way to make his life meaningful. It's kinda like a Bildungsroman. Typically, I like when authors shift between first and third person pov but it was confusing and almost disjoint at times. 1000% not what I expected this to be but beautifully written.

title: Malibu Rising
author: Taylor Jenkins Reid
review: I enjoyed the book it was a solid read but didn't particularly resonate with me, therefore the mid rating.

title: For One More Day
author: Mitch Albom
review: Interesting… kinda reminded me of when Harry gets "killed" and Dumbledore talks to him in the train station. I love Tuesdays with Morrie but I can't quite articulate my mixed feelings about this one.
